query: definition and existence of negative enhancers in genome biology

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Spores -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Endospores can resist inactivation from ethanol treatment. [3] They can also survive high temperatures up to 150°C, making specific Gram-positive species heat resistant. Further, bacterial spores can show typical viability signs at temperatures near absolute zero. Endospores are resistant to chemical agents, eg, triphenylmethane dyes, and can even protect the bacterial cells against ultraviolet radiation, extreme pH gradients, drought, and nutrition depletion. Endospores germinate back into vegetative cells (an active bacterial cell that undergoes metabolism) when surrounding environmental conditions favor bacterial growth and reproduction. Several stimulants, such as optimal close-to-body temperature and diffusion of nutrients and water through bacterial cell walls by altering their surface tension, revert bacterial cells to their active vegetative cells. The process of spore formation is a multistep process. It starts from bacterial DNA replication, followed by the forespore formation, which is, by definition, the pinching of the cellular plasma membrane between the replicated chromosomes. Then, a cortex forms between the inner and outer membrane by extending the second cellular membrane to enclose the forespore with calcium and dipicolinic acid. Finally, the external spore coat surrounds the endospore before its release. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Duty to Warn -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Genomic database studies may reveal some pathogenic variants and mutations. [16] Genetic mutations do not necessarily imply that the disease undoubtedly occurs. [1] Genetic testing and future prognostications are nowhere crystalline. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Female Development -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The sex of a fetus is due to the sperm provided by the biological father. Each oocyte, from the biological mother, has an X chromosome and does not influence the sex of the fetus. If the father provides another X chromosome the fetus will be female. There is an incredible amount of development that occurs in the uterus, but female development does not stop after birth.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Epigenetic Mechanism -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Briefly, negatively charged DNA is packaged around a positively charged histone protein octamer, which contains 2 copies of histone proteins H2A, H2B, H3, and H4. [2] This nucleoprotein complex is a nucleosome, the basic unit of chromatin. [3] The nucleosomes of a continuous DNA polymer are connected by linker DNA and the complex is stabilized by histone protein H1. The aggregation of chromatin results in the formation of a chromosome. The chromatin of a chromosome exists as either loose, transcriptionally active euchromatin or dense, transcriptionally inactive heterochromatin. [4] Chemical alterations to histone proteins can induce the formation of either the open euchromatin state, which facilitates gene expression by allowing transcription factors and enzymes to interact with the DNA, or the closed heterochromatin state, which suppresses gene expression by preventing initiation of transcription.

retrieved_docs.content: Pompe disease or type 2 glycogen storage disease Glucosamine (UDP-N-acetyl)-2-epimerase/N-acetylmannosamine kinase  (GNE) myopathy, an autosomal recessive disorder. One study reported this sign in 90% of patients with GNE. [10] Tubular aggregate myopathy Myotonic dystrophy Sporadic inclusion body myositis Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis [11] Acid maltase deficiency in an adult patient [12] Autosomal recessive limb-girdle muscular dystrophy type 2G, a telethoninopathy [13] FLAD1 -related lipid storage myopathy [14] Limb-girdle dysferlinopathy with a heterozygous missense variation in exon 10 of the DYSF gene [15] COL6A3 c.6817-2(IVS27)A>G variant associated with Bethlem myopathy [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Physics -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Beta particles subdivide into negative and positive emissions. A negative beta emission has a single negative charge, and it increases the number of protons by one and decreases the number of neutrons by one. A positive beta emission contains a single positive charge, and it decreases the number of protons by one and increases the number of neutrons by one. In both cases, the atomic mass stays the same, but it forms a different element. Unlike alpha particles, beta particles can travel far and penetrate through water and tissue with high energy. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Electric Potential and Capacitance -- Definition/Introduction -- The Concept of Charge
retrieved_docs.content: Electric potential and capacitance originate from the concept of charge. The charge is determined by comparing the number of protons and electrons present in a material. A net positive charge indicates more protons than electrons. Conversely, a net negative charge denotes more electrons than protons. An equal number of protons and electrons indicates a neutral charge. Materials with charge also exhibit electrical forces—opposite charges attract (for example, positive and negative) and similar charges repel (for example, positive and positive or negative and negative). The unit of measurement for the charge is a coulomb (C). Protons and electrons have a charge of +1 or +1.602×10 −19 coulombs and −1 or −1.602×10 −19 coulombs, respectively. These charge values are considered the elementary charge because the accumulation of microscopic electrons and protons determines the macroscopic charge of the material. This concept is pertinent when considering polymers comprised of several materials whose compositions are studied under an electric field. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor suppressor genes are important genes that act within the genome to regulate several cellular functions. These genes can be broadly classified based on their role in cell growth/cell cycle progression, cell proliferation, DNA repair mechanisms, and other crucial cellular signaling functions such as the apoptosis induction. Without functional tumor suppressor genes, there is a high risk of dysregulated cell growth that is a well-known mechanism for the development of cancers. [1] Loss of function mutations in tumor suppressor genes has been identified in many types of cancers, including ovarian, lung, colorectal, head and neck, pancreatic, uterine, breast, and bladder cancer. [2] [3] [4] [5] [6] There are even familial cancer syndromes associated with the loss of function germline mutations of specific tumor suppressor genes like Li-Fraumeni syndrome with the loss of TP53. [7] Extensive research is underway to understand these genes and their relationship to cancers to facilitate the development of novel targets for specific cancer types.

retrieved_docs.title: Pain Theory -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Although inflammation and pain are an essential part of healing (acute pain), in some circumstances, they can lead to negative effects. Several mechanisms involving the activation of peripheral and central sensitization pathways through the sensitization of peripheral nociceptors, and alterations in spinal dorsal horn neurons, and central nervous system (CNS) brain areas, can trigger a pathogenetic cascade that ends with the development of chronic pain. Again, pieces of evidence suggest the paramount role of the environment (i.e., epigenetic) and genetics. [2] [3] Thus, the chronicity of pain is the effect of changes in pain processing through transcription and transduction processes. As a consequence, chronic pain is no longer simply a symptom but becomes itself a disease. Currently, in the United States, estimates are that 100 million individuals suffer from the negative effects of protracted courses of pain and inflammation. [3] This epidemic is growing at an alarmingly fast rate and is taking a significant toll on the economy as well as patients and providers. In fact, there are reports that the cost for chronic pain patients is anywhere between 560 to 635 billion dollars annually. [3] Furthermore, increased opioid prescription and use, until opioid addiction in the United States and Canada is a dramatic phenomenon that has been responsible for up to 70.000 drug overdose deaths, in 2017. Although it appears that in other countries (e.g., Western Europe) the data is not so alarming. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Lambda Waves -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Essentially, this signal is generated by pyramidal cells located in the upper layers of the cerebral cortex. EEG represents the expression of synaptic processes (pre- and postsynaptic electrical potentials), dendritic potentials, and probably also neuroglia potentials. The mathematical law describing the conduction of electric potential from pyramidal neurons to the detection surface is known as the Poisson equation. This law relates the surface distribution of potential to the charge underlying it and the permittivity of the tissue mass. Because pyramidal cells behave like dipole sources, they produce the so-called "dipolar fields"; the total field generated results from the linear combination of the potential fields that each source produces individually. The presence of a dipole of this type requires a significant population of depolarized neurons in unison to produce external potential. This phenomenon is defined as local synchrony. The role of local synchrony in EEG rhythm generation is so intense that less than 5 percent of pyramidal cells can be responsible for over 90 percent of the energy of the EEG signal.

retrieved_docs.title: Short-Term Memory Impairment -- Introduction -- Short-Term and Long-Term Memory
retrieved_docs.content: In neurofunctional terms, the difference is the occurrence in long-term memory of a series of events that must definitively fix the engram(s). This effect occurs through the establishment of neural networks and is expressed as a neurofunctional phenomenon, including long-term potentiation, which is an increase in the strength of the neural transmission deriving from the strengthening of synaptic connections. This process requires gene expression and the synthesis of new proteins and is related to long-lasting structural alterations in the synapses (synaptic consolidation) of the brain areas involved, such as the hippocampus in declarative memories.

retrieved_docs.title: Rectal Cancer -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The NCCN and the College of American pathologists, among other associations, recommend universal MMR/MSI status (15 to 20% sporadic CRC), BRAF p.V600 E (less than 10% sporadic CRC), and RAS mutational (12 to 75% sporadic CRC) testing for prognostic and predictive of chemotherapy efficacy.  hMLH1 and hMSH2 IHC provides a high sensitive (92.3%), and specific (100%) method, and positive (96.7%)  / negative (100%) predictive value method for screening for MMR/MSI status. MSI-H is synonymous with dMMR, and MSI-stable will refer to proficient MMR state. The prevalence of MSI-H in CRC is about 15% in stage II, 8% in stage III, and 4% to 5% in stage IV. BRAF gene mutation testing is indicated after a negative MLH1 IHC, indicating MLH1 gene down-regulation through somatic methylation. Recently, 6 independent classification systems coalesced into 4 consensus molecular subtypes (CMSs) with distinguishing features: CMS1 (MSI-immune, 14%), hypermutated burden, dMMR, microsatellite unstable and strong immune activation; CMS2 (canonical, 37%), high chromosomal instability, epithelial, marked WNT and MYC signaling activation; CMS3 (metabolic, 13%), epithelial and evident metabolic dysregulation, KRAS mutation; and CMS4 (mesenchymal, 23%), CpG hyper-methylation, prominent transforming growth factor-beta activation, stromal invasion, and angiogenesis. CMS classification had a prognostic value, CMS1 good, CMS4 poor, and CMS2/3 intermediate. [9] [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Chromosome Abnormalities -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Chromosomal abnormalities generally fall into two basic categories: numerical or structural. Numerical disorders are considerably more common than structural ones; they involve any deviation from the normal diploid number for a given species. This category can further subdivide into a group in which individual chromosomes may be either missing or duplicated and a group in which whole haploid sets of chromosomes may be added or lost. [1] In contrast, structural abnormalities involve the genomic rearrangement of one or more chromosomes. Most structural aberrations result from the unequal exchange between chromosomes or enzymatic misrepair of two chromosome breakages. [4] Examples of this disorder include deletions, translocations, and inversions, duplications, ring chromosomes, and isochromosomes. Table 1 displays a list of definitions and examples of chromosomal abnormalities according to their classification (numerical or structural).

retrieved_docs.title: Lambda Waves -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The cerebral cortex contains tens of billions of neurons organized into functional groups; these groups are interconnected through a complex series of connections between cortical regions and subcortical brain structures. During the normal brain function, these networks are subjected to rhythmic activity at frequencies ranging from 1 to 100 Hz and more. Although the underlying neuronal activity proceeds at a frequency of thousands of Hz, the measurable external potentials are all in the EEG range. These groups of cortical neurons undergo cycles of activity in which they get sequentially recruited. These potential fluctuations are characterizable in terms of spectral content or characteristics in the time domain. Indeed, this coordinated activity is characterized by rhythmic waves that are discernible in specific positions and are evident under particular conditions (for example, during sleep) or after stimulation (hyperventilation, light stimulation, sleep deprivation, evoked potentials). This cyclical pattern of activity produces an identifiable rhythm of growth and decline, which exhibits a temporal trend of the order of seconds and shows significant variability.

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Laboratory -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cytometry is a technique to measure the properties of individual cells, such as size, shape, and DNA content, which can help diagnose and monitor conditions like cancer or genetic disorders. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Inducible Operon -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanisms by which transcription regulation is determined can be positive or negative. If the regulatory protein is an activator, we can say it is a positive regulation; if the protein is a repressor, the regulation is negative. We can distinguish inducible operons with negative and positive regulation and repressible operons with positive and negative regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of a liveborn infant encompasses a range of perinatal classifications that are linked through gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth status. A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a specific child category of liveborn, indicating that infants born alive at this stage are included within the broader liveborn group. The duration of gestation has an inverse‑isa relationship with liveborn, reflecting that shorter gestational periods are associated with distinct clinical classifications of liveborn infants. One major classification of liveborn infants is short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This category both classifies liveborn and is self‑referential, emphasizing its central role in describing infants born with reduced gestational length, low birth weight, or impaired growth. The disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight is positioned as a parent of liveborn, indicating that this disorder underlies the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 173
retrieved_docs.content: Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96) constitute a broad diagnostic category that includes, as a child entity, Respiratory and cardiovascular disorders specific to the perinatal period (P19‑P29). Within the same overarching group, the condition Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation is linked as a parent concept, indicating that it is a more specific manifestation of perinatal disorders. This condition is classified both as تقييد النمو الجنيني (fetal growth restriction) and as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (extremely low birth weight, unspecified), reflecting its dual characterization in terms of growth limitation and birth‑weight severity. The concept تقييد النمو الجنيني has a broader relationship to حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة (unspecified condition originating in the perinatal period), suggesting that fetal growth restriction is part of a larger, less defined set of perinatal abnormalities. In

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 5 -- Community 2928
retrieved_docs.content: In embryology, the set of permitted descriptors—referred to as “Allowed qualifier”—includes the entities بيلة سِّيسْتينِية, فالج, and قرحات الطرفين السفليين, indicating that these specific conditions are recognized as relevant qualifiers within the study and classification of embryologic development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2270
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical presentation described as “Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation” is used to define and classify that same set of perinatal abnormalities, and it is categorized under the term “تقييد النمو الجنيني,” which translates to fetal growth restriction. This grouping emphasizes that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively recognized as a single entity of fetal growth restriction in medical classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2810
retrieved_docs.content: Newborn Screening Terminology is classified as a concept within the plod subset, indicating that it belongs to the plod collection of terms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 977
retrieved_docs.content: Newborn Screening Terminology is a categorical framework that incorporates several obstetric and perinatal concepts. Within its subset, it includes the Arabic terms مقدمات تسمم الحمل (preliminary signs of preeclampsia), تسمم الحمل (preeclampsia), and قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (mild hyperemesis gravidarum), reflecting maternal conditions that can affect newborn health and screening outcomes. Additionally, the subset of Newborn Screening Terminology encompasses the concept الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين (delivery by fetal suction or vacuum‑assisted extraction), highlighting a specific mode of birth that may be relevant to newborn assessment and follow‑up. Together, these concepts illustrate how Newborn Screening Terminology integrates maternal disease states and delivery techniques to inform comprehensive newborn evaluation and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: The notion of an “Allowed qualifier” within the field of virologie designates specific Arabic terms that are recognized as permissible descriptors in virological contexts. In particular, the expressions “قيلة مثانية” and “ضُمورُ الكَبِد الأصفر الحادّ” are identified as allowed qualifiers in virologie, indicating that these phrases can be used to qualify or describe virology‑related observations or conditions. Recognizing these qualifiers helps ensure precise terminology and consistent communication when documenting virological findings that involve the concepts represented by “قيلة مثانية” and “ضُمورُ الكَبِد الأصفر الحادّ”.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 5 -- Community 2943
retrieved_docs.content: In enzymology, the concept of an “Allowed qualifier” encompasses specific terms that are recognized as permissible descriptors, including the Arabic expressions وضعية المَجيء المقعدي, نوبة, and متلازمة الضائقة الجنينية. These qualifiers are accepted within enzymological contexts to denote particular conditions or states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: The NICHD Terminology contains a specific subset in which the concept الهَذَيان is included, and this same subset also encompasses the concepts الْتِهابُ المَشيماءِ والسَّلَى, vakcíny proti chřipce, and التهاب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِي جرثومي. This organization demonstrates that the NICHD Terminology groups together a diverse range of medical topics, linking the Arabic‑language term الهَذَيان with other Arabic concepts of placental and related inflammation, the Czech term for influenza vaccines, and an Arabic term for bacterial intestinal infection, thereby reflecting its broad coverage of disease entities and preventive interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2800
retrieved_docs.content: In the field of virology, the concept of an allowed qualifier is applied to the terms التموت and ورم عضلي أملس, indicating that these Arabic descriptors are recognized as permissible qualifiers within virological contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1616

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2033
retrieved_docs.content: In genetic terminology, the category “Allowed qualifier” is linked by the relationship “genetika” to both فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ and متلازمة الضائقة الجنينية, indicating that these two entities are recognized as permissible genetic qualifiers.

In addition, an inverse‑is‑a relationship links the entity “سرطانة توتية” to the primary malignant tumor morphology (“ورم خبيث أولي”), further emphasizing its classification as a type of primary malignant neoplasm. Together, these relationships illustrate how various primary malignant neoplasms across different organs are unified by common pathological processes, shared morphological descriptors, and hierarchical links to broader cancer concepts, aiding in consistent classification and clinical understanding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is directly linked to the outcome of a liveborn infant, indicating that reaching this stage of pregnancy can result in a newborn who is alive at birth. In the broader classification of pregnancy timelines, the concept of Duration of gestation encompasses liveborn as a specific category or outcome, reflecting that livebirth is considered one of the possible results within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of gestational age in determining the likelihood of a live birth.
